topics.title: Biomechanics of Hard Tissues: Structure and Mechanical Properties of Bone, Cortical and Cancellous Bones
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT612 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Biomechanics of Hard Tissues: Structure and Mechanical Properties of Bone, Cortical and Cancellous Bones

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Biomechanics of hard tissues** is the branch of applied mechanics that quantitatively analyses the *structure*, *composition*, and *load-bearing response* of mineralized connective tissues — primarily **bone** — when subjected to physiological, pathological, or external mechanical loads. The discipline treats bone as a **biphasic, anisotropic, hierarchical, and viscoelastic composite material** whose macroscopic behaviour emerges from a precisely organized micro/nano-architecture.

Bone is fundamentally a **two-phase composite**:
- An **organic matrix** (≈ 30–35 % by weight; mainly Type I collagen + proteoglycans) that provides **toughness, ductility, and tensile strength**.
- A **inorganic mineral phase** (≈ 60–65 %; primarily hydroxyapatite, $Ca_{10}(PO_4)_6(OH)_2$) that imparts **stiffness and compressive strength**.
- ≈ 5–10 % **water**, critical for viscoelastic and poroelastic behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"Bone is a living, anisotropic, viscoelastic, hierarchical composite biomaterial whose mechanical properties are functions of its composition, apparent density, porosity, trabecular architecture, and loading history."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of bone as a **reinforced concrete building**:

| Component of Bone | Real-World Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Hydroxyapatite (mineral) | Concrete / bricks | Resists compression, provides stiffness |
| Type I collagen (organic) | Steel rebar | Resists tension, prevents brittle fracture |
| Trabeculae (cancellous bone) | Internal scaffolding / trusses | Distributes loads, absorbs energy |
| Cortical shell | Outer concrete wall | Resists bending and torsion |
| Bone marrow / Haversian canals | Hollow elevator shaft | Houses living cells, lightens structure |

Just as an engineer reinforces concrete with steel to obtain a material strong in **both tension and compression**, nature reinforces hydroxyapatite with collagen to obtain bone — a material that is simultaneously **stiff, tough, lightweight, and self-repairing**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Did you know?** A human femur can withstand a compressive load of **≈ 800–1500 N** repeatedly (during walking) and a one-time ultimate load of **≈ 6000–8000 N** before fracture — roughly **30× body weight**.

## 1.3 Hierarchical Structural Organization (Macro → Nano)

Bone exhibits a **7-level structural hierarchy**, each contributing to the overall mechanical response:

1. **Macrostructure** — whole bone (e.g., femur, vertebra)
2. **Mesostructure** — cortical vs. cancellous regions
3. **Microstructure** — Haversian systems (osteons), trabeculae (100 µm – 1 mm)
4. **Sub-microstructure** — lamellae (3–7 µm)
5. **Nanostructure** — mineralized collagen fibrils (≈ 100–200 nm diameter)
6. **Sub-nanostructure** — individual mineral crystals, tropocollagen molecules
7. **Molecular** — amino-acid sequence, HA lattice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This hierarchy is *not* cosmetic — it is the reason bone is **damage-tolerant**. A crack propagating at the micro-scale is arrested/deflected by the lamellar interfaces, preventing catastrophic failure at the macro-scale.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Major Bone Types: Cortical vs. Cancellous

### 2.1.1 Cortical (Compact) Bone
- Forms the **dense outer shell** of all bones and the **diaphysis** of long bones.
- Composed of tightly packed **osteons (Haversian systems)**.
- **Porosity** ≈ **5–10 %** (Haversian & Volkmann canals, lacunae).
- **Apparent density** ≈ **1.85 g/cm³**.
- Primary role: **resist bending, torsion, and direct compression**.

### 2.1.2 Cancellous (Trabecular / Spongy) Bone
- Found in **epiphyses**, **vertebral bodies**, and the **inner metaphysis**.
- Composed of a 3-D lattice of **trabeculae** (struts 50–300 µm thick).
- **Porosity** ≈ **70–90 %** — hence "apparent" density is far lower.
- **Apparent density** ≈ **0.1 – 1.0 g/cm³** (varies with anatomical site).
- Primary role: **distribute loads, absorb impact energy, house marrow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Apparent density** = mass of whole specimen ÷ *bulk* volume (including pores). **True density** ignores pores. The two are different by a factor of porosity.

## 2.2 Mechanical Loading Modes

Bone in vivo is subjected to:

- **Tension** — pulling apart (e.g., anterior femur during stair descent)
- **Compression** — pushing together (e.g., vertebral body under body weight)
- **Shear** — sliding parallel planes (e.g., tibial plateau under femoral condyles)
- **Bending** — combination of tension (outer fibre) + compression (opposite fibre)
- **Torsion** — twisting about the long axis (e.g., shaft of femur during rotation)
- **Combined / Multiaxial** — physiological loading is *never* uniaxial

## 2.3 Stress–Strain Behaviour — The Engineering Core

For any load mode, the fundamental continuum mechanics relations are:

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} \quad \text{[Pa]} \qquad \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}
$$

For linear elastic response (Hookean region):

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where **E** = Young's modulus (stiffness). Bone is **anisotropic**, so *E* is a **4th-order tensor** with 21 independent constants in the most general case; by symmetry, this reduces for bone.

For **transverse isotropy** (typical bone model), **5 elastic constants** suffice:

$$
E_L,\ E_T,\ G_{LT},\ \nu_{LT},\ \nu_{TT}
$$

where subscripts *L* (longitudinal) and *T* (transverse) refer to the bone's long axis.

## 2.4 Anisotropy Ratio

$$
\rho_E = \frac{E_L}{E_T}
$$

For cortical bone, **$\rho_E \approx 1.5$ to 2.0**, meaning bone is **stiffer along its long axis** than perpendicular to it — a direct consequence of osteon alignment.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Memorization)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit | Applicable to |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Stress | MPa, GPa | All loading modes |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Strain | dimensionless (or %) | All loading modes |
| 3 | $\sigma = E\,\varepsilon$ | Hooke's law | — | Linear-elastic region |
| 4 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's modulus | **17–20 GPa (cortical-L)** | Cortical bone |
| 5 | $G = E / \left[ 2(1+\nu) \right]$ | Shear modulus | ≈ 3–6 GPa | Isotropic estimate |
| 6 | $K = E / \left[ 3(1-2\nu) \right]$ | Bulk modulus | ≈ 8–15 GPa | Hydrostatic response |
| 7 | $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_{ax}$ | Poisson's ratio | **0.25–0.40** (cortical) | Lateral contraction |
| 8 | $\sigma_{uts}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | **50–150 MPa** | Cortical |
| 9 | $\sigma_{ucs}$ | Ultimate compressive strength | **130–200 MPa** | Cortical |
| 10 | $\sigma_y$ | Yield strength | ≈ 60 % of UTS | Cortical |
| 11 | $\tau_{uts}$ | Ultimate shear strength | **≈ 53 MPa** | Cortical (≈ 60 % of tensile) |
| 12 | $U_r = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma\, d\varepsilon$ | Modulus of resilience | J/m³ | Toughness precursor |
| 13 | $U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma\, d\varepsilon$ | Modulus of toughness | J/m³ | Energy to fracture |
| 14 | $E^* = E_s\, \rho_r^{\,2}$ | Cancellous power law | — | Cancellous bone |
| 15 | $\sigma_{uts}^* = C\, \rho_r^{\,n}$ | Cancellous strength-density | $n \approx 1.8$ | Cancellous bone |
| 16 | $K_t = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{nom}$ | Stress concentration factor | ≥ 1 (function of geometry) | Holes, notches |
| 17 | $Z = \sigma_{uts} / E$ | Modulus index | ≈ 0.007 (cortical) | Material selection |
| 18 | $\rho_{app} = m / V_{bulk}$ | Apparent density | g/cm³ | Distinguishes cortical/cancellous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Equation 14 & 15** (Carter & Hayes, 1977 type laws): For cancellous bone, mechanical properties scale with **apparent density raised to a power** between **1.5 and 2.5**. This is **THE** single most important relationship for KTU numerical questions on cancellous bone.
>
> Reference: $E = C_1\, \rho_{app}^{2}$ and $\sigma_{uts} = C_2\, \rho_{app}^{\,n}$, where typical constants are $C_1 \approx 2.5$ GPa·(cm³/g)² and $C_2 \approx 35$ MPa·(cm³/g)ⁿ.

## 2.6 Viscoelasticity of Bone

Bone is **not** purely elastic — it exhibits:

- **Creep** — increasing strain under constant stress
- **Stress relaxation** — decreasing stress under constant strain
- **Strain-rate sensitivity** — stiffer and stronger at higher loading rates

A common simplified model uses the **standard linear solid (Zener) model** with parameters $E_1, E_2, \eta$. KTU may ask a *qualitative* question comparing elastic vs. viscoelastic response.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Orthopaedic implant design** | Stem stiffness must match host bone to avoid *stress shielding* |
| **Prosthetic limb design** | Socket–bone interface load transfer |
| **Forensic biomechanics** | Fracture pattern analysis to determine load direction/magnitude |
| **Sports medicine** | Bone remodelling in athletes (Wolff's law quantification) |
| **Finite Element Modelling** | Patient-specific CT-based $E$ and $\sigma_y$ mapping |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Young's Modulus from Stress–Strain Slope

**Problem Statement:** A cylindrical specimen of cortical bone of gauge length 30 mm and cross-sectional area 25 mm² is loaded in tension. A force of 500 N produces an elongation of 0.045 mm. Calculate Young's modulus in the linear-elastic region.

### Step 1 — Identify the given data
$$
L_0 = 30\ \text{mm},\quad A = 25\ \text{mm}^2 = 25\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2,\quad F = 500\ \text{N},\quad \Delta L = 0.045\ \text{mm} = 0.045\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute engineering stress
$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{500\ \text{N}}{25\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma = \frac{500}{25}\times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 20\times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 20\ \text{MPa}
$$

*Logic:* Stress is internal force per unit cross-sectional area. We express in MPa ($10^6$ Pa) because bone stiffnesses are typically reported in GPa and strengths in MPa.

### Step 3 — Compute engineering strain
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.045\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}}{30\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}} = 0.0015
$$

*Logic:* Strain is the *fractional* change in length. 0.0015 corresponds to 0.15 % — well within the linear-elastic region of cortical bone.

### Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law to obtain E
$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{20\times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}}{0.0015}
$$

$$
E = 13.33\times 10^{9}\ \text{Pa} = 13.33\ \text{GPa}
$$

### Step 5 — Compare to literature & interpret
- The computed modulus (13.33 GPa) is in the **transverse direction range** for cortical bone.
- A **longitudinal-direction** cortical specimen of the same material would yield $E \approx$ 17–20 GPa — confirming bone's **anisotropy** (a transverse cortical test gives lower stiffness).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** If a question does not state "longitudinal" or "transverse," a KTU-correct answer must mention that the value is *direction-dependent* and quote a range, not a single number.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cancellous Bone Modulus from Apparent Density (Carter–Hayes Power Law)

**Problem Statement:** A cancellous bone specimen has an apparent density of **0.4 g/cm³**. Using the empirical relation $E^{*} = 2.5\, \rho_{app}^{2}$ (with $E$ in GPa and $\rho$ in g/cm³), estimate the apparent Young's modulus. Comment on its load-bearing capability compared to cortical bone.

### Step 1 — Substitute values
$$
E^{*} = 2.5\ \text{GPa}\cdot(\text{cm}^3/\text{g})^2 \times (0.4\ \text{g/cm}^3)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Square the density
$$
(0.4)^2 = 0.16\ (\text{g/cm}^3)^2
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply
$$
E^{*} = 2.5 \times 0.16 = 0.40\ \text{GPa} = 400\ \text{MPa}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare to cortical
$$
\frac{E_{cortical}}{E_{cancellous}} \approx \frac{17\ \text{GPa}}{0.4\ \text{GPa}} \approx 42.5
$$

*Logic:* Cancellous bone at 0.4 g/cm³ is **≈ 40× less stiff** than cortical bone. However, the *trabecular architecture* aligns with principal stress lines (Wolff's law), so the effective in-vivo stiffness in those directions is much higher than the off-axis values.

### Step 5 — Ultimate strength estimate
Using $\sigma_{uts}^* = 35\ \rho_{app}^{1.8}$:

$$
\sigma_{uts}^* = 35\ \text{MPa}\cdot(\text{cm}^3/\text{g})^{1.8} \times (0.4)^{1.8}
$$

$$
(0.4)^{1.8} = e^{1.8\,\ln 0.4} = e^{1.8 \times (-0.9163)} = e^{-1.649} \approx 0.192
$$

$$
\sigma_{uts}^* \approx 35 \times 0.192 = 6.72\ \text{MPa}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Property Table (KTU Must-Know)

| Property | Cortical Bone (Longitudinal) | Cortical Bone (Transverse) | Cancellous Bone (typical, $\rho=0.4$ g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Apparent density** | 1.85 g/cm³ | 1.85 g/cm³ | 0.1 – 1.0 g/cm³ |
| **Porosity** | 5–10 % | 5–10 % | 70–90 % |
| **Young's modulus (E)** | **17–20 GPa** | 6–13 GPa | **0.05 – 0.5 GPa** |
| **Ultimate tensile strength** | 50–150 MPa | — | 1–10 MPa |
| **Ultimate compressive strength** | 130–200 MPa | — | 2–12 MPa |
| **Yield strain** | ≈ 0.6–0.7 % | ≈ 0.5 % | 0.5–1.0 % |
| **Failure strain** | ≈ 1.5–3.0 % | ≈ 0.6–1.5 % | ≈ 5–10 % |
| **Poisson's ratio (ν)** | 0.30 – 0.40 | 0.25 – 0.35 | 0.20 – 0.45 |
| **Shear modulus (G)** | 3.1 – 5.7 GPa | 2.4 – 4.5 GPa | 0.05 – 0.4 GPa |
| **Ash fraction** | ≈ 60–65 % | ≈ 60–65 % | ≈ 35–50 % |
| **Haversian canals present?** | Yes | Yes | No (trabecular only) |
| **Primary load mode** | Bending, torsion, compression | Transverse compression | Energy absorption, distributed loading |
| **Anatomical site** | Diaphysis of long bones, outer shell | Diaphysis (radial direction) | Epiphyses, vertebral bodies, metaphysis |

## 3.4 Derivation: Stress Concentration at a Drill Hole (Engineering Implication)

A surgeon drilling into cortical bone creates a circular hole (a stress-riser). For a plate of width $w$ containing a central hole of diameter $d$, the classical Kirsch solution (1898) gives:

$$
K_t = 3.00 - 3.13\left(\frac{d}{w}\right) + 3.66\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^2 - 1.53\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^3
$$

For a small hole ($d/w \ll 1$):

$$
K_t \to 3.0
$$

Hence, even a small drill hole **triples the local stress** in surrounding bone. This is why orthopaedic surgeons minimize hole diameter and avoid placing them in high-tension regions. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question**.

### Worked numerical:
For $d = 4\ \text{mm}$ in a cortical strip $w = 20\ \text{mm}$:

$$
\frac{d}{w} = 0.20
$$

$$
K_t = 3.00 - 3.13(0.20) + 3.66(0.04) - 1.53(0.008)
$$

$$
K_t = 3.00 - 0.626 + 0.1464 - 0.01224 = 2.508
$$

So the maximum stress at the hole edge is **≈ 2.5× the nominal stress** away from the hole.

---
