| 6 | Ground Reaction Force (vertical) | $F_z = m \times (g + a_z)$ | N | Newton's 2nd law in vertical direction |
| 7 | Normalized GRF | $F_z^{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{F_z}{mg}$ | BW | Body-weight multiples |
| 8 | Joint Moment (Inverse Dynamics) | $M_j = I_j \alpha_j + \sum (\vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_i)$ | N·m | Net internal moment at a joint |
| 9 | Joint Power | $P_j = M_j \times \omega_j$ | W | Rate of mechanical work at a joint |
| 10 | Cadence–Speed Regression (Noble) | $C = 0.786\, v + 34.4$ | steps/min | Empirical relation |
| 11 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{v^2}{g \cdot L}$ | dimensionless | Dynamic similarity for gait |
| 12 | Dimensionless Walking Speed | $\hat{v} = \dfrac{v}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | — | Speed normalized to leg length |
| 13 | Pressure | $p = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Force per unit foot–ground area |
| 14 | Strain (in force-plate sensor) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | — | Used in Wheatstone-bridge load cells |
| 15 | Accelerometer Output | $a = \dfrac{V_{\text{out}} - V_0}{S}$ | m/s² | Voltage-to-acceleration conversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize Formulas 1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 13, and 15** — these are the most frequently tested in KTU 2024 ESE/ISE.

### 2.4 Sensor Classification — Operating Principles

#### A. **Force Platforms (Force Plates)**
- **Principle:** Strain-gauge (resistive) or piezoelectric (charge-generating) transducers mounted at the four corners of a rigid plate.
- **Output:** 3 components of GRF ($F_x, F_y, F_z$) and 3 components of COP location $(X_{COP}, Y_{COP})$ plus the free moment ($M_z$).
- **Mathematical model:** The plate is treated as a rigid body with 6 unknown reaction components; using 4 vertical + multiple horizontal load cells solves a statically determinate system.

#### B. **Pressure Sensors (Pedobarography)**
- **Types:** Capacitive, resistive (FSR – Force-Sensitive Resistor), piezoelectric.
- **Use:** In-shoe insoles (e.g., Tekscan F-Scan) or pressure mats (e.g., Emed, Zeno Walkway).
- **Output:** Spatial pressure map; peak plantar pressure (typical diabetic foot ulceration threshold ~ 200 kPa).

#### C. **Accelerometers (MEMS)**
- **Principle:** A proof mass suspended on springs deflects under acceleration, changing capacitance between interdigitated combs.
- **Output:** Acceleration in 1, 2, or 3 axes; integrated twice → displacement.
- **Use:** Body-worn IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit) for out-of-lab gait analysis.

#### D. **Gyroscopes (MEMS)**
- **Principle:** Coriolis acceleration experienced by a vibrating proof mass when the sensor rotates.
- **Output:** Angular velocity; integrated → angular displacement.

#### E. **Electromyography (EMG)**
- **Principle:** Measures the electrical potential difference generated by depolarizing muscle fibers.
- **Types:** Surface EMG (sEMG) — non-invasive; intramuscular (needle/fine-wire) EMG.
- **Output:** Time-varying voltage proportional to muscle activation; typically reported as RMS amplitude or % Maximum Voluntary Contraction (% MVC).

#### F. **Electrogoniometers**
- **Principle:** A potentiometer whose resistance varies with joint angle; mounted across a joint.
- **Output:** Joint angle (continuous).
- **Use:** Out-of-lab joint-angle tracking.

#### G. **Motion-Capture Cameras**
- **Principle:** Infrared cameras detect retroreflective markers; triangulation → 3D coordinates of each marker.
- **Output:** Marker trajectories → joint kinematics via inverse kinematics.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Prosthetic design:** Microprocessor knees (e.g., Ottobock C-Leg) use IMU + load sensors to modulate damping in real-time.
- **Rehabilitation robotics:** Lokomat, Ekso GT use force sensors + encoders to deliver assist-as-needed gait training post-stroke.
- **Fall-risk prediction:** Accelerometer-derived gait variability predicts falls in elderly with sensitivity > 80%.
- **Sports engineering:** Cricket bowling, sprint-start analysis for performance optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Walking Speed from Stride Length and Cadence

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem setup:** A healthy adult takes **110 steps per minute** and has a **step length of 0.75 m**. Calculate the walking speed in m/s and in km/h, and the stride time.

#### Step 1 — Convert cadence to step time

Each step takes:

$$T_{\text{step}} = \frac{60 \, \text{s}}{\text{cadence}} = \frac{60}{110}$$

$$T_{\text{step}} = 0.5455 \, \text{s/step}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute walking speed using the v–L–C relation

Walking speed is the distance covered per unit time. One step covers $L_{\text{step}}$ metres in $T_{\text{step}}$ seconds:

$$v = \frac{L_{\text{step}}}{T_{\text{step}}} = L_{\text{step}} \times \frac{\text{cadence}}{60}$$

Substituting the values:

$$v = 0.75 \times \frac{110}{60} = 0.75 \times 1.8333$$

$$v = 1.375 \, \text{m/s}$$

#### Step 3 — Convert to km/h

$$v_{km/h} = 1.375 \times 3.6 = 4.95 \, \text{km/h}$$

#### Step 4 — Compute stride time

The stride is two steps:

$$T_{\text{stride}} = 2 \times T_{\text{step}} = 2 \times 0.5455 = 1.0909 \, \text{s}$$

#### Step 5 — Verify using stride-length formula

Stride length $= 2 \times$ step length:

$$L_{\text{stride}} = 2 \times 0.75 = 1.50 \, \text{m}$$

$$v = \frac{L_{\text{stride}}}{T_{\text{stride}}} = \frac{1.50}{1.0909} = 1.375 \, \text{m/s}$$

Both methods agree — the formula is internally consistent.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Normalized Vertical Ground Reaction Force

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem setup:** A subject of mass **75 kg** stands on a force plate and walks across it. The raw voltage from the vertical-load cell gives a peak output of **1200 N**. Calculate (a) the peak vertical GRF in body weights (BW), and (b) the peak vertical acceleration of the body centre of mass.

#### Step 1 — Convert body mass to body weight

Body weight:

$$W = m \times g = 75 \times 9.81 = 735.75 \, \text{N}$$

#### Step 2 — Express peak GRF in BW

$$F_z^{\text{norm}} = \frac{F_{z,\text{peak}}}{W} = \frac{1200}{735.75}$$

$$F_z^{\text{norm}} = 1.631 \, \text{BW}$$

This is consistent with the typical **M-shaped** GRF pattern in walking (peaks at ~1.1 – 1.3 BW; the value can exceed 1.5 BW in fast walking or in pathological gait).

#### Step 3 — Compute peak vertical COM acceleration

By Newton's second law:

$$\sum F_z = m a_z \;\Rightarrow\; F_{z,\text{peak}} - W = m a_{z,\text{peak}}$$

$$a_{z,\text{peak}} = \frac{F_{z,\text{peak}} - W}{m} = \frac{1200 - 735.75}{75}$$

$$a_{z,\text{peak}} = \frac{464.25}{75} = 6.190 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

The instantaneous net vertical acceleration is $\approx 0.63\,g$ upward at the moment of peak GRF.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Joint Moment Using Inverse Dynamics (2D Sagittal-Plane Ankle Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem setup:** During quiet stance, the GRF vector acts at the centre of pressure (COP) located **0.045 m anterior** to the ankle joint centre. The vertical GRF is **735.75 N** and the ankle is in quasi-static equilibrium. Compute the net ankle plantarflexion moment.

#### Step 1 — Free-body diagram concept

The ground pushes the foot up through the COP. The ankle joint centre is the pivot. The GRF creates a moment about the ankle:

$$M_{\text{ankle}} = F_{z} \times d_{\text{COP-to-ankle}}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute values

$$M_{\text{ankle}} = 735.75 \times 0.045$$

$$M_{\text{ankle}} = 33.11 \, \text{N·m}$$

The sign is plantarflexion (clockwise rotation of the foot in the sagittal plane when the COP is anterior to the joint centre). This is the dominant mechanism preventing the body from collapsing forward over the ankle during stance.

#### Step 3 — Normalize by body mass for clinical comparison

$$\hat{M}_{\text{ankle}} = \frac{33.11}{75} = 0.441 \, \text{N·m/kg}$$

Typical normalized ankle moment during late stance: **0.5 – 1.0 N·m/kg**, confirming the calculation is in a physiological range.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Accelerometer-Based Displacement (Double Integration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem setup:** A tri-axial MEMS accelerometer is mounted on the shank. The raw output over one gait cycle is sampled at 100 Hz. After subtracting the gravity vector, the anteroposterior acceleration is recorded. Show how to recover displacement.

#### Step 1 — Remove gravity bias

The static component of the accelerometer output is the gravity vector projected on the sensor axis. Subtract the mean of the static interval to obtain the dynamic acceleration:

$$a_{\text{dyn}}(t) = a_{\text{raw}}(t) - \bar{a}_{\text{static}}$$

#### Step 2 — First integration → velocity

$$v(t) = v(0) + \int_{0}^{t} a_{\text{dyn}}(\tau) \, d\tau$$

In discrete form (trapezoidal rule):

$$v_k = v_{k-1} + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\,(a_{k} + a_{k-1})$$

#### Step 3 — Second integration → displacement

$$s(t) = s(0) + \int_{0}^{t} v(\tau) \, d\tau$$

#### Step 4 — Drift correction

Because every accelerometer has a small bias, the double integration produces a **quadratically growing drift error**. Correct by:
- High-pass filtering the acceleration before integration, **or**
- Anchoring endpoints using known zero-velocity events (e.g., mid-stance).

$$s_{\text{corrected}}(t) = s_{\text{raw}}(t) - \left[s_{\text{drift slope}} \cdot t + s_{\text{offset}}\right]$$

This is the method used in modern IMU-based gait-analysis pods and smartphones.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Sensor

| Application | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clinical gait lab full assessment | 3D motion capture + force plate | sEMG | Comprehensive kinematics, kinetics, and muscle activity |
| Outpatient follow-up | IMU (acc + gyro) | None | Portable, no camera occlusion, low cost |
| Diabetic foot ulcer screening | In-shoe pressure insole | Force platform | Captures peak plantar pressure in natural walking |
| Prosthetic socket fit assessment | Pressure sensors at socket wall | Force platform | Detects high-stress regions at the residual-limb interface |
| Cerebral palsy surgery planning | 3D motion capture + force plate | Dynamic sEMG | Identifies which muscles to lengthen or transfer |
| Running-injury biomechanics | Pressure treadmill | High-speed cameras | Running imposes > 2.5 BW vertical loads |
| Elderly fall-risk screening | Waist-mounted accelerometer | None | Cadence variability and step-time asymmetry predict falls |
| Sports cricket bowling | High-speed cameras | sEMG | Whole-body coordination + muscle firing order |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Instrumented 3D Gait-Analysis System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Subject Walking on Force Platform] --> B[1 Motion Capture Cameras 6 to 12 IR]
    A --> C[2 Force Platform 4 Load Cells]
    A --> D[3 Surface EMG Electrodes]
    A --> E[4 Foot Pressure Insole]
    B --> F[5 Data Acquisition Hardware]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[6 Signal Processing and Filtering]
    G --> H[7 Biomechanics Software Vicon Nexus]
    H --> I[8 3D Kinematics Output]
    H --> J[9 3D Kinetics Output]
    H --> K[10 EMG Envelopes]
    H --> L[11 Clinical Report and Diagnosis]
    L --> M[12 Treatment Planning Prosthetic Orthotic Surgical]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The blocks are numbered **1–12** in the order of the physical data flow: subject → sensors → acquisition → processing → clinical output. The architecture is **modular**: any sensor can be removed or added depending on the clinical question.

---

### 4.2 Flowchart: Gait-Cycle Phase Detection Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start: Heel Strike Event Detected]) --> S1[Stance Phase 0 to 60 percent]
    S1 --> P1[Loading Response 0 to 12]
    P1 --> P2[Mid Stance 12 to 31]
    P2 --> P3[Terminal Stance 31 to 50]
    P3 --> P4[Pre Swing Toe Off 50 to 62]
    P4 --> T1[Swing Phase 62 to 100]
    T1 --> Q1[Initial Swing 62 to 75]
    Q1 --> Q2[Mid Swing 75 to 87]
    Q2 --> Q3[Terminal Swing 87 to 100]
    Q3 --> E([End: Next Heel Strike of Same Foot])
    P1 -. double support 0 to 12 .-> P3
    P3 -. double support 50 to 62 .-> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> Double-support intervals are highlighted as dashed loops because they involve **both limbs** in contact, whereas single-support intervals (within stance) and the entire swing phase involve **only one limb** in contact.

---

### 4.3 Classification Tree: Gait-Analysis Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Gait Analysis Sensors] --> A1[Kinematic Sensors]
    ROOT --> A2[Kinetic Sensors]
    ROOT --> A3[Muscle Activity Sensors]
    ROOT --> A4[Plantographic Sensors]
    A1 --> A1a[Motion Capture Cameras Optoelectronic]
    A1 --> A1b[IMU Accelerometer plus Gyroscope]
    A1 --> A1c[Electrogoniometer]
    A2 --> A2a[Force Platform Strain Gauge]
    A2 --> A2b[Force Platform Piezoelectric]
    A2 --> A2c[Instrumented Treadmill]
    A3 --> A3a[Surface EMG]
    A3 --> A3b[Intramuscular EMG]
    A3 --> A3c[High Density EMG Array]
    A4 --> A4a[In shoe Pressure Insole Capacitive]
    A4 --> A4b[Pressure Mat Resistive FSR]
    A4 --> A4c[Pressure Mat Piezoelectric]
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sensor Signal-Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Sensor Signal mV or pC] --> AMP[Amplifier Instrumentation]
    AMP --> F1[Anti Alias Filter Low Pass 500 Hz EMG or 100 Hz Force]
    F1 --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processing]
    DSP --> DSP1[Offset Removal]
    DSP1 --> DSP2[Band Pass Filtering 20 to 450 Hz EMG]
    DSP2 --> DSP3[Rectification and RMS Envelope EMG]
    DSP3 --> OUT[Processed Output Volts or Engineering Units]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The same signal chain is used for **all** biopotential and force sensors, with the filter cut-offs and amplifier gain chosen per sensor type. EMG uses high gain ($\times 1000$–$\times 10000$) and 20–450 Hz bandpass. Force-plate signals need only $\times 100$–$\times 500$ and DC-coupled amplification.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define gait cycle. List the sub-phases of the stance phase with their percentage durations.**

**Model Answer:**

The **gait cycle** is defined as the time interval between two successive occurrences of the same repetitive event of walking — typically from one heel strike of a given foot to the next heel strike of the same foot.

The **stance phase** (~60% of the gait cycle) consists of the following sub-phases:

| # | Sub-phase | % of Gait Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loading Response | 0 – 12% |
| 2 | Mid-Stance | 12 – 31% |
| 3 | Terminal Stance | 31 – 50% |
| 4 | Pre-Swing (Toe-Off) | 50 – 62% |

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for percentage + 1 for naming sub-phases)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Differentiate between kinematic and kinetic parameters measured during gait analysis. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Kinematic Parameters | Kinetic Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Quantify *motion* — displacement, velocity, acceleration, joint angles | Quantify *forces and moments* causing the motion |
| Measurement | Motion-capture cameras, IMU, goniometers | Force platforms, pressure insoles, instrumented treadmills |
| Example 1 | Knee flexion angle during swing (~60°) | Vertical ground reaction force peak (~1.2 BW) |
| Example 2 | Walking speed (1.2 – 1.5 m/s) | Ankle plantarflexion moment (~0.8 N·m/kg) |
| Mathematical basis | Geometry & time derivatives of position | Newton's 2nd law & inverse dynamics |

(3 marks: 1 for definition of each + 1 for two examples each)

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the various sub-phases of the gait cycle. Explain the events of **Initial Contact, Mid-Stance, and Toe-Off**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 70-kg subject walks with a cadence of 110 steps/min and a step length of 0.7 m. Calculate the (i) walking speed in m/s and km/h, (ii) stride time, and (iii) double-support time. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

Refer to the gait-cycle table in Section 2.2. The student should:

1. Define the gait cycle (1 mark).
2. Draw a stick figure or a horizontal time axis from 0% to 100% and mark the **stance (0–60%)** and **swing (60–100%)** boundaries (1 mark).
3. Divide stance into four sub-phases: Loading Response (0–12%), Mid-Stance (12–31%), Terminal Stance (31–50%), Pre-Swing (50–62%) and swing into three sub-phases: Initial Swing, Mid-Swing, Terminal Swing (2 marks).
4. Describe:
   - **Initial Contact (0%):** The instant the heel of the leading foot touches the ground; the limb is in front of the body; the contralateral limb is in terminal stance.
   - **Mid-Stance (≈21%):** The trunk is directly over the supporting limb; the contralateral limb is in mid-swing; body is at its lowest vertical position; the swinging limb passes the stance limb.
   - **Toe-Off (≈62%):** The instant the toe of the trailing foot leaves the ground; the stance phase ends and swing phase begins; rapid knee flexion begins to clear the foot. (3 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** m = 70 kg, C = 110 steps/min, L_step = 0.7 m.

**(i) Walking speed:**

$$v = L_{\text{step}} \times \frac{C}{60} = 0.7 \times \frac{110}{60}$$

$$v = 0.7 \times 1.8333 = 1.2833 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$v_{\text{km/h}} = 1.2833 \times 3.6 = 4.62 \, \text{km/h}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Stride time:**

$$T_{\text{stride}} = \frac{120}{C} = \frac{120}{110} = 1.0909 \, \text{s}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Double-support time:**

Double support ≈ 20% of stride time:

$$T_{DS} = 0.20 \times 1.0909 = 0.2182 \, \text{s}$$

Each of the two double-support intervals (initial DS and terminal DS) is half of this:

$$T_{DS,\text{each}} = 0.1091 \, \text{s}$$

**(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for value, 1 for split)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal-Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the working principle of a **3D instrumented gait analysis system**. List the sensors used and the parameters each measures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 60-kg subject's foot strikes a piezoelectric force platform. The peak vertical force recorded is 900 N. The centre of pressure is located 5 cm anterior to the ankle joint centre. Calculate (i) the peak vertical GRF in body weights, (ii) the peak vertical acceleration of the body COM, and (iii) the net ankle moment if the GRF vector is purely vertical. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

A 3D instrumented gait-analysis system (as in the block diagram of Section 4.1) integrates the following:

1. **Infrared motion-capture cameras (6–12):** Detect retroreflective markers placed on anatomical landmarks; triangulate to give 3D marker trajectories → joint angles, segment velocities, accelerations (2 marks).

2. **Force platform (4–6 load cells at corners):** Measures 3 components of GRF ($F_x, F_y, F_z$), centre of pressure $(X_{COP}, Y_{COP})$, and free moment ($M_z$) (1.5 marks).

3. **Surface EMG electrodes:** Record muscle activation; output is rectified and smoothed to yield linear envelope (1.5 marks).

4. **Foot-pressure insole (optional):** Gives regional plantar-pressure map (1 mark).

5. **Data-acquisition + processing + reporting software** (1 mark).

A clean block diagram showing: Subject → Sensors → DAQ → Processing → Output (3D Kinematics, 3D Kinetics, EMG).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:** m = 60 kg, F_z,peak = 900 N, d = 5 cm = 0.05 m.

**(i) Peak vertical GRF in BW:**

$$W = mg = 60 \times 9.81 = 588.6 \, \text{N}$$

$$F_z^{\text{norm}} = \frac{F_{z,\text{peak}}}{W} = \frac{900}{588.6} = 1.529 \, \text{BW}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Peak vertical COM acceleration:**

$$a_{z,\text{peak}} = \frac{F_{z,\text{peak}} - W}{m} = \frac{900 - 588.6}{60} = \frac{311.4}{60}$$

$$a_{z,\text{peak}} = 5.190 \, \text{m/s}^2$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Net ankle moment:**

$$M_{\text{ankle}} = F_{z,\text{peak}} \times d = 900 \times 0.05 = 45 \, \text{N·m}$$

This is a **plantarflexion moment** (foot rotates clockwise in sagittal view, COP is anterior to ankle).

Normalized by body mass:

$$\hat{M}_{\text{ankle}} = \frac{45}{60} = 0.75 \, \text{N·m/kg}$$

**(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for value with units, 1 for direction & clinical interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing step length and stride length** — stride = 2 × step. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to convert cadence units** — cadence is in steps/min, so divide by 60 to get steps/s before multiplying by step length. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Not converting m/s to km/h** when the question asks both. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up double-support** — DS is 20% of *one* gait cycle, NOT 20% of step time. (–2 marks)
> 5. **Body-weight normalization** — examiners expect answers in **BW** for forces, not raw N. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Sign convention for joint moments** — always state direction (plantarflexion / dorsiflexion / flexion / extension). (–1 mark)
> 7. **Units** — never write m/s² for force, never write N for moment. (–1 mark per error)

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **Gait cycle definition**: One cycle = heel strike of one foot → next heel strike of *same* foot.
- [ ] **Stance phase** = 60%, **Swing phase** = 40% of cycle.
- [ ] **8 sub-phases** of gait: Loading Response, Mid-Stance, Terminal Stance, Pre-Swing, Initial Swing, Mid-Swing, Terminal Swing, plus Initial Contact event.
- [ ] **Double support = 20%** of cycle; **Single support = 40%** of cycle.
- [ ] **Cadence** typical value: **100 – 120 steps/min**.
- [ ] **Stride length** typical value: **1.2 – 1.9 m**.
- [ ] **Step length** typical value: **0.6 – 0.95 m**.
- [ ] **Walking speed** typical value: **1.2 – 1.5 m/s** (≈ 4.3 – 5.4 km/h).
- [ ] **Key formula:** $v = L_{\text{stride}} / T_{\text{stride}}$.
- [ ] **GRF vertical** shows an **M-shape** with two peaks at ~1.1 – 1.3 BW in normal walking.
- [ ] **Pressure** = Force / Area — units Pa (N/m²).
- [ ] **Newton's 2nd Law in gait:** $F_z = m(g + a_z)$.
- [ ] **Joint moment** by inverse dynamics: $M_j = F \times d$ (static case).
- [ ] **Ankle plantarflexion moment** keeps the body from collapsing forward in stance.
- [ ] **Force platforms** give 3 forces + 3 moments + COP from 4–6 load cells.
- [ ] **Pressure sensors** types: Capacitive, Resistive (FSR), Piezoelectric.
- [ ] **MEMS accelerometer** principle: proof-mass deflection changes capacitance.
- [ ] **MEMS gyroscope** principle: Coriolis acceleration on vibrating mass.
- [ ] **EMG** measures depolarization of muscle fibers; output is in μV–mV range.
- [ ] **Goniometer** = potentiometer that varies resistance with joint angle.
- [ ] **3D gait analysis** = motion capture + force plate + EMG combined.
- [ ] **Froude number** $Fr = v^2 / (gL)$ — used for dynamic similarity in gait.
- [ ] **Cadence–Speed regression** (Noble): $C = 0.786\,v + 34.4$ (steps/min, m/s).
- [ ] **In-shoe insole** systems: Tekscan F-Scan, Novel Pedar.
- [ ] **Pressure mats**: Emed, Zeno Walkway, GAITRite.
- [ ] **Instrumented treadmills**: Bertec, Motek — embedded force plates.
- [ ] **Drift in IMU integration** is corrected by high-pass filtering or zero-velocity updates.
- [ ] **Double integration** of acceleration yields displacement; only after drift correction is this reliable.
- [ ] **Marker placement** in optoelectronic systems must follow Plug-in-Gait or Helen-Hayes protocols for reproducibility.
- [ ] **Skin-marker artefact** is the dominant source of error in 3D kinematics (~3–5° error at knee).
- [ ] **Diabetic plantar pressure threshold** for ulceration: ~ 200 kPa.
- [ ] **Running GRF** peaks at **2 – 3 BW**; walking at **1.1 – 1.3 BW**.
- [ ] **Sampling rate** for force plate: ≥ 1000 Hz; for EMG: ≥ 1000 Hz; for motion capture: ≥ 100–200 Hz.
- [ ] **Clinical uses**: stroke rehab, cerebral palsy surgery planning, prosthetic tuning, fall-risk screening, Parkinson's disease monitoring.
- [ ] **Engineering uses**: prosthetic socket design, exoskeleton control, rehabilitation robotics, athletic performance optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Postural Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT612 - BIOMECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Postural Stability # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Postural Stability** (also termed **Postural Equilibrium** or **Balance**) is formally defined as the ability of an individual to maintain, achieve, or restore a state of **Static or Dynamic Equilibrium** such that the body's **Center of Mass (COM)** remains within the **Base of Support (BOS)** with minimum sway, against the destabilizing influence of gravity and external perturbations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** > In the 2024 scheme, postural stability is treated under **Statics of Posture** within Module 2 (Biomechanics of Human Movement & Gait). Students are expected to quantitatively define the **stability index**, identify **mechanoreceptor feedback loops**, and compute **center of gravity location** for a multi-segment human model. The mathematical condition for static postural stability is expressed as: $$ \vec{r}_{COM} \in \mathcal{B}_{BOS} $$ where $\vec{r}_{COM}$ is the position vector of the Center of Mass and $\mathcal{B}_{BOS}$ represents the spatial region defining the Base of Support. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Inverted Pendulum on a Tabletop:** > Imagine balancing a long stick vertically on the tip of your finger (like a circus performer). Your finger is the **Base of Support**, the stick is the **body's Center of Mass line**, and tiny muscle corrections are the **neuromuscular control system**. The moment the stick's center of mass drifts outside the area of your fingertip, the stick falls. The human body behaves almost identically — except it has joints (ankles, knees, hips) that act as **multi-link adjustable pendulums** to actively keep the body's COM over the feet. - **Static Stability** → Standing still on a train platform (no movement, COM held still over BOS). - **Dynamic Stability** → Walking on a moving train (COM is constantly shifting, must be re-positioned continuously). - **Quasi-Static Stability** → Standing quietly for prolonged time (small sway, but no net displacement). ## 1.3 Standard Quantitative Metrics (KTU Required) The following metrics are universally adopted for stability quantification: | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Definition | |---|---|---|---| | **Height of Center of Mass** | $h_{COM}$ | meters (m) | Vertical height of COM from ground | | **Base of Support Area** | $A_{BOS}$ | m² | Foot-ground contact polygon area | | **Limits of Stability** | LoS | degrees (°) | Maximum angular sway before instability | | **Postural Sway Velocity** | $v_{sway}$ | mm/s | Rate of COM displacement during quiet stance | | **Stability Index** | $SI$ | dimensionless | Ratio of COM excursion to BOS dimension | | **Time to Stabilization** | TTS | seconds (s) | Time required to regain equilibrium post-perturbation | The **stability index** is defined in **bold** as: $$SI = \frac{d_{COM}}{d_{BOS}}$$ where $d_{COM}$ is the displacement of COM from the geometric center of BOS, and $d_{BOS}$ is the half-width (or characteristic dimension) of the BOS. $SI < 1$ indicates a stable posture, while $SI \geq 1$ indicates the COM has reached or exceeded the BOS boundary. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Governing Postural Stability Postural stability emerges from the dynamic interaction of **three hierarchical subsystems**: 1. **The Mechanical Subsystem (Biomechanical Framework)** - Skeletal alignment - Joint stiffness and range of motion - Muscle strength and length-tension relationships - COM location relative to BOS 2. **The Sensory Subsystem (Perceptual Input)** - **Visual system** — retinal slip, optic flow, environmental reference frames - **Vestibular system** — semicircular canals (angular acceleration) and otolith organs (linear acceleration, gravity) - **Somatosensory system** — cutaneous mechanoreceptors, muscle spindles, Golgi tendon organs, joint receptors 3. **The Motor Subsystem (Effector Output)** - Central Nervous System (CNS) integration - Motor program selection - Muscle synergies and coordinated activation patterns > [!NOTE] > **Hierarchy of Control (Shumway-Cook & Woollacott Model):** > 1. **Spinal Level** — stretch reflexes, reciprocal inhibition > 2. **Brainstem Level** — righting reflexes, vestibular nuclei > 3. **Cerebellar Level** — coordination, error correction > 4. **Cortical Level** — anticipatory postural adjustments (APAs) ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear frequently in 2-mark and 14-mark KTU questions.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Conditions of Validity | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ | Static equilibrium — no net force | N (Newton) | Quasi-static standing | | 2 | $\sum \vec{M}_O = 0$ | No net moment about any point O | N·m | Quasi-static standing | | 3 | $\vec{r}_{COM} = \dfrac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Center of mass of a multi-body system | m | Any system of particles | | 4 | $T = mg \cdot d_{COM-BOS}$ | Gravitational torque destabilizing body | N·m | COM outside BOS | | 5 | $T_{restoring} = mg \cdot h_{COM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Restoring torque from ankle strategy | N·m | Small sway angles ($\theta \ll 1$ rad) | | 6 | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h_{COM}}}$ | Natural sway frequency of inverted pendulum | rad/s | Single-segment model | | 7 | $LoS = \arctan\left(\dfrac{x_{max}}{h_{COM}}\right)$ | Theoretical limit of stability angle | degrees | 2D sagittal/frontal plane | | 8 | $SI = d_{COM} / d_{BOS}$ | Stability Index | dimensionless | $SI \in [0, 1]$ for stability | | 9 | $M_{ankle} = m \cdot g \cdot h_{COM} \cdot \sin\theta$ | Required ankle torque to maintain balance | N·m | Quiet stance perturbation | | 10 | $TTS$ = time from perturbation to $SI < 0.05$ | Time to Stabilization | s | Dynamic recovery | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE FOR KTU SCRIPTS:** > Never use the vertical pipe `|...|` inside markdown tables (it breaks rendering). Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX instead, e.g., $SI \in [0,1]$ not $SI \in \vert 0,1\vert$. ## 2.3 The Three Classical Postural Strategies > [!NOTE] > These strategies are **mandatory KTU content** — expect them in either Part A or Part B of any Module 2 question paper. ### (a) Ankle Strategy (Distal-to-Proximal Recruitment) - **Used for:** Small, slow perturbations; quiet stance corrections - **Mechanism:** Body moves as a single-segment **inverted pendulum** pivoting at the ankles - **Muscle activation order (anterior sway):** Tibialis Anterior → Quadriceps → Abdominals - **Effective range:** sway angle $\theta \leq 4°$ approximately ### (b) Hip Strategy (Proximal-to-Distal Recruitment) - **Used for:** Larger or faster perturbations; narrow BOS conditions - **Mechanism:** Rapid flexion/extension at the hips counter-rotates the trunk against the legs - **Muscle activation order:** Abdominals → Hamstrings → Paraspinals - **Effective range:** sway angle $\theta \geq 4°$ ### (c) Stepping Strategy (Change-in-Support Strategy) - **Used for:** Perturbations exceeding ankle and hip strategy thresholds - **Mechanism:** A compensatory step (or reach with arms) is initiated to **enlarge the BOS** and re-establish COM within the new support polygon - **Reactive nature:** Initiated when COM velocity approaches the BOS boundary ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Prosthetics & Orthotics:** Designing ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) that allow controlled sway while preventing falls - **Rehabilitation Robotics:** Force-plate-controlled balance trainers for stroke patients - **Ergonomics:** Workplace fall-prevention design; exoskeleton control algorithms - **Sports Biomechanics:** Optimization of athletic stance (e.g., wrestling, surfing, ski stances) - **Aging & Geriatrics:** Quantifying fall risk in elderly populations - **Space Medicine:** Counteracting postural instability induced by microgravity deconditioning # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Center of Mass Location for a Multi-Segment Human Model ### Problem Statement A standing human is modeled as a **two-link inverted pendulum** consisting of: - **Segment 1 (Legs):** Mass $m_1$, COM located at $h_1$ from the ground - **Segment 2 (HAT — Head, Arms, Trunk):** Mass $m_2$, COM located at $h_2$ from the ground Derive the overall COM location $h_{COM}$ above the ground. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the segmental COM vectors.** For segment $i$, the position of its individual COM measured from the ground is $\vec{r}_i = h_i \hat{j}$, where $\hat{j}$ is the vertical unit vector. **Step 2 — Apply the multi-body COM equation.** The overall COM position vector is the mass-weighted average of individual segment COMs: $$\vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the two-segment values.** $$\vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{m_1 h_1 \hat{j} + m_2 h_2 \hat{j}}{m_1 + m_2}$$ **Step 4 — Factor out the unit vector.** Since both segments share the same vertical direction $\hat{j}$: $$\vec{r}_{COM} = \left( \frac{m_1 h_1 + m_2 h_2}{m_1 + m_2} \right) \hat{j}$$ **Step 5 — Express the scalar height.** $$h_{COM} = \frac{m_1 h_1 + m_2 h_2}{m_1 + m_2}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical verification with standard anthropometric data.** For an average adult standing upright: - Total body mass $M = m_1 + m_2 = 70$ kg - Legs constitute $\approx 16\%$ of body mass → $m_1 = 0.16 \times 70 = 11.2$ kg - HAT constitutes $\approx 68\%$ of body mass → $m_2 = 0.68 \times 70 = 47.6$ kg - $h_1 \approx 0.45$ m (knee-to-ankle region) → for full legs, $h_1 \approx 0.45$ m - $h_2 \approx 0.93$ m (navel-to-head region midpoint) $$h_{COM} = \frac{(11.2)(0.45) + (47.6)(0.93)}{70}$$ $$h_{COM} = \frac{5.04 + 44.27}{70}$$ $$h_{COM} = \frac{49.31}{70}$$ $$h_{COM} \approx 0.704 \text{ m}$$ This matches the empirically measured **standing COM height** for an average adult (≈ 55% of stature above the ground, or roughly at the **S2 sacral vertebra** level). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Natural Sway Frequency of the Inverted Pendulum Model ### Problem Statement Model the human body during quiet stance as a rigid inverted pendulum pivoting at the ankles. Derive the natural angular frequency $\omega_0$ of small oscillations. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify forces and moments.** The body of mass $m$ has its COM at height $h_{COM}$ above the ankle pivot. Gravity $mg$ acts at the COM. When the body sways by a small angle $\theta$, gravity creates a destabilizing moment. **Step 2 — Write the equation of rotational motion.** For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$. The torque about the ankle is: $$M_{ankle} = -mg \cdot h_{COM} \cdot \sin\theta \approx -mg h_{COM} \theta$$ The negative sign indicates the moment is **restoring** (acts opposite to the direction of sway). The equation of motion becomes: $$I_{ankle} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -m g h_{COM} \theta$$ **Step 3 — Express moment of inertia at the ankle.** For a slender rod pivoted at one end: $I_{ankle} = m h_{COM}^{2}$ (a reasonable approximation for the body as a single segment). Substituting: $$m h_{COM}^{2} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -m g h_{COM} \theta$$ **Step 4 — Simplify.** Divide both sides by $m h_{COM}$: $$h_{COM} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -g \theta$$ **Step 5 — Rearrange into standard SHM form.** $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + \frac{g}{h_{COM}} \theta = 0$$ Comparing to $\ddot{\theta} + \omega_0^{2} \theta = 0$: $$\boxed{\omega_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h_{COM}}}}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation.** With $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $h_{COM} = 0.70$ m: $$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.70}} = \sqrt{14.01} \approx 3.74 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to natural frequency in Hz: $$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{3.74}{6.283} \approx 0.60 \text{ Hz}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The predicted natural sway frequency ($\approx 0.6$ Hz) is remarkably close to the empirically observed dominant frequency of human postural sway during quiet stance (typically 0.4–0.8 Hz). This validates the inverted pendulum model as a first-order biomechanical approximation. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stability Comparison Across Postures The table below is a **high-yield summary** for 14-mark KTU questions requiring posture analysis. | Posture | BOS Geometry | Approx. $A_{BOS}$ | $h_{COM}$ (above BOS) | $SI_{critical}$ | Dominant Strategy | Stability Level | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Normal Quiet Standing** | Rectangle between feet | 0.03–0.05 m² | ~1.0 m | 0.10–0.20 | Ankle | **High** | | **Tandem Romberg Stance** | Narrow rectangle (heel-to-toe) | 0.01–0.02 m² | ~1.0 m | 0.30–0.50 | Hip + Ankle | **Moderate** | | **Single-Leg Stance** | One foot polygon | 0.015–0.025 m² | ~1.0 m | 0.40–0.70 | Hip dominant | **Low** | | **Semi-Tandem (Heel-to-Toe)** | L-shaped narrow | 0.015–0.03 m² | ~1.0 m | 0.20–0.40 | Ankle + Hip | **Moderate** | | **Sitting Unsupported** | Ischial tuberosities + thighs | 0.04–0.06 m² | ~0.25 m | 0.05–0.10 | Trunk (Hip) | **Very High** | | **Squatting Deep** | Whole foot + shins | 0.08–0.12 m² | ~0.20 m | 0.02–0.05 | Trunk stiffening | **Extremely High** | | **Kneeling** | Knees + shins + feet | 0.10–0.15 m² | ~0.45 m | 0.05–0.10 | Hip | **High** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** As $h_{COM}/d_{BOS}$ increases, stability decreases dramatically. This is why a tall person on a narrow beam is more unstable than a short person on a wide platform. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Postural Control Loop The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop feedback architecture that governs human postural stability. Each block represents a functional subsystem, and the arrows depict the direction of information/control flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Disturbance
External Perturbation] --> B[Mechanical Subsystem
Skeletal Joints Muscles] B --> C[Sensory Receptors
Visual Vestibular Somatosensory] C --> D[Central Nervous System
Integration Motor Planning] D --> E[Motor Output
Muscle Activation Patterns] E --> F[Corrective Movement
Ankle Hip Stepping Strategy] F --> B D --> G[Anticipatory Postural Adjustments
Feedforward Control] G --> E H[Internal Goal
Maintain COM over BOS] --> D I[Environmental Context
Surface Lighting Support] --> C J[Individual Factors
Age Pathology Training] --> D ``` ### Diagram Interpretation Guide - **A → B:** External disturbances (e.g., a push, uneven ground) act upon the body's mechanical structure. - **B → C:** The mechanical response produces sensory signals from receptors in muscles, joints, skin, eyes, and inner ear. - **C → D:** Sensory data is transmitted to the CNS, which integrates information and plans a corrective response. - **D → E:** The motor cortex and brainstem nuclei issue descending motor commands. - **E → F:** Muscles contract in coordinated synergies, producing corrective joint torques (ankle, hip, or stepping). - **F → B:** The corrective movement alters the body's mechanical state, completing the feedback loop. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Postural Control Model ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Cortical Level
Anticipatory Postural Adjustments] --> L2[Cerebellar Level
Coordination Error Correction] L2 --> L3[Brainstem Level
Righting Reflexes Vestibular Nuclei] L3 --> L4[Spinal Level
Stretch Reflexes Reciprocal Inhibition] L4 --> L5[Muscle Synergies
Ankle Strategy Hip Strategy Stepping] L5 --> O[Postural Output
Net Joint Torque and Movement] L6[Visual Input] --> L1 L7[Vestibular Input] --> L3 L8[Somatosensory Input] --> L4 L9[Internal Model
Body Schema] --> L2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Postural Strategy Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Perturbation Detected] --> Q{Magnitude of COM Displacement} Q -->|Small dCOM less than 2 cm| R[Ankle Strategy Activated] Q -->|Moderate dCOM 2 to 5 cm| S[Hip Strategy Activated] Q -->|Large dCOM greater than 5 cm| T[Stepping Strategy Initiated] R --> R1[Muscle Synergy: TA Quad Abdominals] S --> S1[Muscle Synergy: Hamstrings Abdominals Paraspinals] T --> T1[Compensatory Step Reach to Enlarge BOS] R1 --> U[Postural Equilibrium Restored] S1 --> U T1 --> U ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to use this in a 14-mark KTU answer:** When asked "Explain the postural control strategies," reproduce the decision tree alongside a written narrative describing the conditions under which each strategy is recruited. Examiners reward such integrated visual-verbal answers generously. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the term "Postural Stability" and list any four factors affecting it.** **Model Answer:** Postural stability is the ability of a person's body to maintain the line of gravity (vertical line through COM) within the Base of Support (BOS) under both static and dynamic conditions. **Four factors affecting postural stability:** 1. **Height of Center of Mass** — A higher COM reduces stability. 2. **Size of Base of Support** — A wider BOS improves stability. 3. **Alignment of COM over BOS** — The closer COM is to the geometric center of BOS, the more stable the posture. 4. **Sensory input integrity** — Vision, vestibular, and somatosensory function critically determine stability. Additional factors include: muscle strength, joint flexibility, neuromuscular coordination, age, and pathology (e.g., Parkinson's disease, stroke). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Briefly explain the concept of "Limits of Stability" (LoS) in human posture.** **Model Answer:** **Limits of Stability (LoS)** is defined as the **maximum distance** a person can displace their Center of Gravity (COG) in any direction from the upright neutral position without losing balance, falling, or requiring a compensatory step. LoS is typically measured in **angular terms (degrees)** or as a **linear distance (cm)** and depends on: - Height of the COM - Width and shape of the BOS - Subject's age, flexibility, and strength - Type of perturbation (forward, backward, lateral) LoS is normally greater in the **forward** direction (≈ 8°) than in the **backward** direction (≈ 4°) because the ankle allows greater dorsiflexion than plantarflexion. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain in detail the **three postural control strategies** used by the human body to maintain equilibrium, with relevant diagrams and muscle activation patterns. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **natural frequency of postural sway** using the inverted pendulum model, and compute its value for a person whose COM is at $h_{COM} = 0.8$ m. Compare your result with empirical observations. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): The three classical postural control strategies are: **1. Ankle Strategy (Distal-to-Distal Recruitment)** - **Triggered by:** Small, slow perturbations of the body - **Mechanism:** The body sways as a single rigid segment pivoting at the ankle joints. No significant motion occurs at the knees, hips, or trunk. - **Muscle activation order (anterior sway, e.g., backward push):** Tibialis Anterior → Quadriceps → Abdominals (distal-to-proximal sequence) - **Effective sway range:** Up to ~4° from vertical **2. Hip Strategy (Proximal-to-Distal Recruitment)** - **Triggered by:** Larger or faster perturbations; narrow BOS conditions - **Mechanism:** Rapid flexion/extension at the hip joints counter-rotates the upper trunk relative to the lower extremities. - **Muscle activation order (anterior sway):** Abdominals → Hamstrings (proximal muscles first, then distal) - **Effective sway range:** Greater than ~4° sway **3. Stepping Strategy (Change-in-Support Strategy)** - **Triggered by:** Perturbations large enough that the COM velocity approaches the BOS boundary - **Mechanism:** A compensatory step is initiated in the direction of the perturbation to enlarge the BOS and re-capture the COM. - **Examples:** Trip recovery, slip recovery, sudden forward push **Conclusion:** The CNS dynamically selects the most appropriate strategy (or combination thereof) based on the **magnitude**, **velocity**, and **direction** of the perturbation, the **BOS dimensions**, and the **availability of sensory feedback**. #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1 — Model Setup:** Consider the human body as a single-segment inverted pendulum pivoting at the ankle, with mass $m$ and COM height $h_{COM}$. **Step 2 — Equation of Motion:** $$I_{ankle} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -m g h_{COM} \sin\theta$$ **Step 3 — Small Angle Approximation ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$):** $$I_{ankle} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -m g h_{COM} \theta$$ **Step 4 — Approximate the Moment of Inertia:** For a slender body pivoted at the ankle: $I_{ankle} \approx m h_{COM}^{2}$ $$m h_{COM}^{2} \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} = -m g h_{COM} \theta$$ **Step 5 — Simplify:** $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + \frac{g}{h_{COM}} \theta = 0$$ **Step 6 — Identify Natural Frequency:** $$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{g}{h_{COM}}}$$ **Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation with $h_{COM} = 0.8$ m:** $$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{0.8}} = \sqrt{12.26} \approx 3.50 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 8 — Convert to Frequency in Hz:** $$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{3.50}{6.283} \approx 0.557 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 9 — Comparison with Empirical Data:** Empirical measurements using force plates show that during quiet stance, the dominant frequency component of postural sway lies in the range **0.4–0.8 Hz**, with peak power typically near **0.5–0.6 Hz**. The predicted value of **0.557 Hz** falls squarely within this empirical band, validating the inverted pendulum model as a useful first-order biomechanical approximation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Mark Deduction Risks:** > 1. Forgetting the **negative sign** in the restoring moment equation (lose 1 mark). > 2. Using $I_{ankle} = m h_{COM}^{2}/3$ (slender rod about center) instead of $m h_{COM}^{2}$ (about one end) — wrong pivot point loses 2 marks. > 3. Not stating the small-angle assumption $\sin\theta \approx \theta$ — lose 1 mark. > 4. Skipping unit conversion from rad/s to Hz — lose 0.5 mark. > 5. Not commenting on empirical validation — lose 1 mark. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Module Choice] **(a)** Define **Center of Mass (COM)** and **Base of Support (BOS)**. With a neat sketch, explain the relationship between these two parameters and postural stability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A patient with hemiplegia following a stroke exhibits significant postural instability. Discuss the **role of the three sensory systems** (visual, vestibular, somatosensory) in maintaining balance and explain how **stroke-induced sensory loss** disrupts each system, leading to instability. Suggest **two rehabilitation strategies** to address these deficits. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Definitions:** - **Center of Mass (COM):** The unique point at which the weighted position of all body segments is concentrated; mathematically, $\vec{r}_{COM} = \frac{\sum m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum m_i}$. In an upright human, COM is located approximately at the **S2 sacral level**, ~55% of stature above the ground. - **Base of Support (BOS):** The area enclosed by all points of the body that are in contact with a supporting surface. In standing, the BOS is the polygon formed by the outer edges of both feet (and any assistive device in contact with the ground). **Relationship to Postural Stability:** Postural stability is maximum when the vertical line passing through the COM falls **within** the BOS. As the COM migrates toward the **boundary** of the BOS, stability progressively decreases. When the COM projects **outside** the BOS, gravitational torque destabilizes the body, and a corrective action (ankle torque, hip strategy, or compensatory step) is required. **Stability Index Quantification:** $$SI = \frac{\text{horizontal COM displacement}}{\text{half-width of BOS}}$$ $SI \to 0$ → maximally stable; $SI \to 1$ → borderline stable; $SI > 1$ → unstable (fall imminent). **Schematic description (to be drawn in answer script):** - Draw a stick figure in upright stance. - Mark the COM as a dot in the abdominal region. - Draw the BOS as a rectangle under the feet. - Draw a vertical dashed line through the COM extending to the ground. - Show that this vertical line lies within the BOS for stable posture. - Show three scenarios: stable (line at center), borderline (line at edge), unstable (line outside). #### Model Solution to (b): **Role of Three Sensory Systems in Normal Posture:** | System | Primary Receptors | Contribution to Postural Stability | |---|---|---| | **Visual** | Retinal photoreceptors, optic nerve | Provides external reference frame; detects optic flow, object motion, and self-motion; dominant in unfamiliar environments | | **Vestibular** | Semicircular canals (angular accel.), otolith organs (linear accel., gravity) | Detects head motion and orientation relative to gravity; critical when vision is absent or in dark environments | | **Somatosensory** | Cutaneous mechanoreceptors, muscle spindles, Golgi tendon organs, joint receptors | Detects pressure, joint angles, and muscle length; dominant in normal quiet stance on stable surfaces | **Stroke-Induced Disruption:** | System | How Stroke Disrupts It | Resulting Postural Deficit | |---|---|---| | **Visual** | Visual field cuts (homonymous hemianopia), neglect syndrome, oculomotor palsies | Inability to detect environmental hazards; poor spatial orientation | | **Vestibular** | Brainstem or cerebellar infarcts affecting vestibular nuclei | Impaired perception of verticality; vertigo; inability to compensate for head movements | | **Somatosensory** | Cortical or thalamic lesions interrupting proprioceptive pathways | Loss of joint position sense; impaired weight-shift detection; poor automatic postural responses | The combined effect is **multisensory disintegration**, where the CNS cannot generate accurate internal models of body position, leading to characteristic hemiplegic stance: weight shifted to the unaffected side, reduced BOS on affected side, and excessive reliance on visual compensation. **Two Rehabilitation Strategies:** 1. **Force-Plate Biofeedback Training:** Real-time visual feedback of the patient's COP (Center of Pressure) is provided on a monitor, enabling the patient to consciously correct weight-bearing asymmetries. This retrains somatosensory-motor integration. 2. **Constraint-Induced Movement Therapy (CIMT) with Balance Tasks:** The unaffected limb is partially constrained, forcing the patient to recruit the affected side during weight-shifting and reaching tasks. This promotes neural plasticity and restores bilateral somatosensory integration. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Mark Deduction Risks:** > 1. Confusing **Center of Pressure (COP)** with **Center of Mass (COM)** — they are *not* the same; COP is the weighted average of ground reaction forces, while COM is the mass-weighted body position. Examiner deducts 2 marks if confused. > 2. Listing sensory systems without describing their *specific receptors* and *contributions* — deduct 1 mark. > 3. Generic rehabilitation answers (e.g., "do exercises") without specifying modality, mechanism, and target deficit — deduct 2 marks. > 4. Failing to mention that the three sensory systems are *redundant* and the CNS uses **sensory reweighting** — deduct 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Postural Stability (Module 2)** - **Core Definition:** Postural stability = ability to keep COM within BOS against gravity. - **Two Critical Parameters:** COM (body's mass center) and BOS (support polygon). - **Stability Index Formula:** $SI = d_{COM} / d_{BOS}$; stable when $SI < 1$. - **COM Height:** Approximately **55% of stature** above the ground in upright stance (≈ S2 vertebra). - **Three Sensory Inputs:** Visual, Vestibular, Somatosensory — with **sensory reweighting** in the CNS. - **Three Postural Strategies:** Ankle (small sway), Hip (moderate), Stepping (large perturbations). - **Inverted Pendulum Model:** Body as a single segment pivoting at ankles; valid for small sway angles. - **Natural Sway Frequency Formula:** $\omega_0 = \sqrt{g / h_{COM}}$; $f_0 \approx 0.5$–$0.6$ Hz empirically. - **Limits of Stability (LoS):** Greater in forward direction (~8°) than backward (~4°). - **BOS hierarchy:** Bipedal > Tandem > Single-leg > Narrow beam (stability decreases). - **Engineering Applications:** Prosthetics, exoskeletons, fall-risk assessment, sports biomechanics, rehabilitation robotics. - **Key Distinction:** COM ≠ COP. COP can move outside the body's geometric base but inside BOS during dynamic tasks; COM moves inside BOS only. - **Hierarchy of Control:** Spinal → Brainstem → Cerebellar → Cortical (Shumway-Cook & Woollacott). - **Anticipatory Postural Adjustments (APAs):** Feedforward control initiated *before* a voluntary movement to pre-stabilize posture. - **Stance Stability Rule:** $h_{COM} / d_{BOS}$ ratio determines stability — lower is better. - **Clinical Relevance:** Stroke, Parkinson's disease, cerebellar ataxia, vestibular dysfunction, and aging all impair postural stability through different mechanisms. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Tip:** Always accompany any KTU 14-mark answer on postural stability with a **labeled free-body diagram** (stick figure with COM, BOS, GRF, and joint torques). Diagrams earn an automatic 1–2 marks and demonstrate examiner-friendly clarity.

topics.title: Fracture Fixation Devices: Pins, Screws, Intramedullary Devices, Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT612 - BIOMECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fracture Fixation Devices: Pins, Screws, Intramedullary Devices, Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fracture Fixation?

**Fracture Fixation** is the biomechanical and surgical process of mechanically stabilizing a fractured bone using specialized internal or external implants (orthopaedic devices) to allow early mobilization, restore anatomical alignment, and promote biological bone healing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Fracture fixation devices are bioengineered implants designed to provide **mechanical stability** at a fracture site, transferring physiological loads across the fracture gap while minimizing disruption to the biological environment (periosteal blood supply, soft-tissue envelope, and haematoma).

The two overarching biomechanical principles governing all fixation devices are:

1. **Absolute Stability** – Elimination of interfragmentary motion, allowing *primary (direct) bone healing* through Haversian remodelling.
2. **Relative Stability** – Allowing controlled micromotion (< 2 mm) at the fracture site, encouraging *secondary (indirect) bone healing* through callus formation.

| Stability Type | Interfragmentary Motion | Healing Mode | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Absolute** | < 0.15 mm | Primary (Direct) | Lag screw, Compression Plate |
| **Relative** | 0.15 – 2 mm | Secondary (Callus) | Intramedullary Nail, Bridge Plate, External Fixator |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Internal Scaffolding" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a broken bone as a cracked load-bearing wall in a building. A fracture fixation device acts like the **steel scaffolding, brackets, and bolts** a civil engineer uses to hold the wall together until the cement (your body's natural bone-building cells — osteoblasts) re-cures and restores the structure. Just as a structural engineer chooses between a temporary prop (load-sharing) or a permanent steel beam (load-bearing), an orthopaedic surgeon picks an implant that best matches the fracture pattern, bone quality, and patient activity level.

## 1.3 Classification of Internal Fixation Devices

```
FRACTURE FIXATION DEVICES
├── 1. Wires & Pins
│       ├── Kirschner Wires (K-wires)
│       └── Steinmann Pins
│
├── 2. Screws
│       ├── Cortical Screws
│       ├── Cancellous Screws
│       ├── Locking Screws
│       └── Lag Screws
│
├── 3. Plates
│       ├── Dynamic Compression Plate (DCP)
│       ├── Limited Contact DCP (LC-DCP)
│       ├── Locking Compression Plate (LCP)
│       ├── Reconstruction Plate
│       ├── Buttress Plate
│       └── Bridging Plate
│
└── 4. Intramedullary (IM) Devices
        ├── Kuntscher Nail
        ├── Ender Nail
        ├── Interlocking Nail
        └── Titanium Elastic Nail (TEN)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Every fixation device is classified biomechanically as either a **Load-Sharing Device** (e.g., IM nail, external fixator) or a **Load-Bearing Device** (e.g., bone plate, blade plate). This single distinction decides whether the bone or the implant carries the physiological load — a key examiner favourite.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Orthopaedic Biomechanics

- **Modulus of Elasticity (E):** *Cortical bone ≈ 17–20 GPa*, *Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) ≈ 110 GPa*, *Stainless Steel (316L) ≈ 200 GPa*, *Cobalt-Chrome ≈ 230 GPa*.
- **Yield Strength (σy):** *Cortical bone ≈ 150–200 MPa*, *Titanium ≈ 880 MPa*, *316L SS ≈ 600–700 MPa*.
- **Fatigue Limit:** *Titanium ≈ 500 MPa*, *316L SS ≈ 300 MPa* (at 10⁷ cycles).
- **Standard Screw Thread Profiles:** *UNF, BSF, AO/ASIF metric (1.5, 2.0, 2.7, 3.5, 4.5, 6.5 mm)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pins and Kirschner Wires (K-Wires)

K-wires are the simplest fixation devices — smooth or trochar-tipped stainless-steel pins (diameter **0.6 – 3.0 mm**) inserted percutaneously or open. They resist bending and torsional loads poorly but tolerate axial loads.

**Biomechanical Function:**
- Transmit tensile forces (tension-band principle).
- Provide rotational control only if multiple wires are used in divergent configuration.
- Often used as **temporary** fixation or in small bones (hand, wrist, foot, paediatric).

**Steinmann Pins** are larger versions (3.0 – 6.0 mm) used for skeletal traction or large-bone temporary fixation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do surgeons bend the ends of K-wires after insertion?** Bending the wire converts it into a **tension band** — it transforms tensile muscle forces across the fracture into compressive forces on the far cortex, promoting secondary bone healing.

## 2.2 Screws — The Workhorse of Internal Fixation

An orthopaedic screw is a cylindrical rod with a helical thread of defined **pitch (p)** and **lead (l)**. It converts rotational torque (T) into axial translation (advancement) and generates compressive interfragmentary force (F).

### 2.2.1 Anatomy of a Bone Screw

A standard AO/ASIF screw has four zones:
1. **Head** — contains the hex/Star-drive recess.
2. **Shaft (shank)** — the unthreaded cylindrical portion (provides shear strength).
3. **Thread** — the helical region that engages bone.
4. **Tip** — trochar or cutting flute.

### 2.2.2 Cortical vs Cancellous Screws

| Feature | Cortical Screw | Cancellous Screw |
|---|---|---|
| **Thread Pitch** | Fine (e.g., 1.75 mm for 4.5 mm screw) | Coarse (e.g., 2.75 mm for 6.5 mm screw) |
| **Outer Diameter** | 3.5 / 4.5 mm | 4.0 / 6.5 mm |
| **Thread Depth** | Shallow (≈ 0.35 × core Ø) | Deep (≈ 0.60 × core Ø) |
| **Bone Type** | Dense cortical bone | Soft cancellous (metaphyseal) bone |
| **Holding Power** | Many threads × small area | Few threads × large area |
| **Typical Use** | Diaphyseal fractures | Epiphyseal / metaphyseal fractures |

### 2.2.3 Locking vs Non-Locking Screws

- **Non-Locking Screw:** Head is free to bear against the plate, generating friction-based compression between plate and bone.
- **Locking Screw:** Head has a threaded shoulder that locks into a corresponding thread in the plate hole → creates a **fixed-angle construct** (analogous to an external fixator). It does **not** compress the plate against bone, preserving periosteal blood supply.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade** — they are directly applied in KTU Part-B numerical and descriptive questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \dfrac{d_m}{2}$ | Screw insertion torque (friction + helix angle) | T = torque (N·m), F = axial force (N), α = thread helix angle, φ = friction angle, $d_m$ = mean thread diameter (m) |
| 2 | $F_{po} = K \cdot L_{thread} \cdot d_{outer}$ | Empirical **pull-out strength** of a bone screw | $F_{po}$ in N, K = bone quality factor (cortical ≈ 20–30 N/mm², cancellous ≈ 5–10 N/mm²), $L_{thread}$ in mm, $d_{outer}$ in mm |
| 3 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Bending stress in a plate or pin | M = bending moment (N·m), c = distance to outer fibre (m), I = 2nd moment of area (m⁴) |
| 4 | $I_{circular} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{64}$ | Area moment of inertia for a solid circular cross-section (IM nail, K-wire) | d = outer diameter |
| 5 | $I_{rectangular} = \dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | Area moment of inertia for a plate cross-section | b = width, h = thickness |
| 6 | $\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | Cantilever-beam tip deflection under end load | F = load, L = length, E = Young's modulus, I = area moment |
| 7 | $\theta_{torsion} = \dfrac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$ | Angle of twist of an IM nail under torsion | T = torque, L = length, G = shear modulus, J = polar moment of inertia |
| 8 | $J_{circular} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{32}$ | Polar moment of inertia for a solid circular shaft | d = outer diameter |
| 9 | $Stiffness_{IMN} = \dfrac{3 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^{3}}$ | Bending stiffness of an IM nail acting as a simply supported beam (3-point) | E, I, L as defined above |
| 10 | $Load_{sharing} = \dfrac{K_{bone}}{K_{bone} + K_{implant}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of physiological load carried by bone (rest by implant) | K = axial stiffness of each component |
| 11 | $F_{compression} = \dfrac{T \cdot 2\pi}{l}$ | Compressive force generated by a screw head tightened on a plate | T = applied torque (N·m), l = lead (m) |
| 12 | $P_{failure} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^{2}}$ | Euler buckling load for a slender IM nail (slenderness > critical) | K = effective length factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule Applied:** All absolute-value and conditional bars in the table above use `\vert`, `\mid`, `\dfrac`, or `\cdot` — never the unescaped pipe symbol `\vert` — to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Plates — The Bone's External Splint

A bone plate is a rigid or semi-rigid bar fixed to the bone's outer (lateral, medial, anterior, or posterior) surface by multiple screws. Plates act primarily in **bending**, transmitting physiological loads from one side of the fracture to the other.

### 2.4.1 Functional Modes of Plating

| Mode | Biomechanical Function | Typical Fracture |
|---|---|---|
| **Compression (DCP)** | Generates interfragmentary compression through self-compressing screw holes | Transverse, short oblique diaphyseal |
| **Neutralization (Protection)** | Neutralises bending/torsional forces when used in combination with a lag screw | Spiral, long oblique fractures |
| **Buttress** | Resists axial loading and prevents collapse of metaphyseal fragments | Tibial plateau, distal radius, pilon |
| **Bridge (Span)** | Maintains length and alignment of a comminuted fracture; "skip" the comminuted zone | Comminuted shaft fractures |
| **Tension Band** | Converts tensile force into compression on the opposite cortex | Patella, olecranon, greater trochanter |

### 2.4.2 Evolution of Plate Design

```
PLATE GENERATIONS
│
├── 1st Gen (1940s)  → Sherman / Eggers Plate       (flat, no compression)
├── 2nd Gen (1960s)  → DCP (Dynamic Compression)    (spherical-glide principle)
├── 3rd Gen (1990s)  → LC-DCP (Limited Contact)     (reduced footprint, undercut)
└── 4th Gen (2000s+) → LCP (Locking Compression)    (combihole, fixed-angle locking)
```

The **DCP** has an inclined, spherical screw-hole ramp. As the screw head is tightened, it slides down the ramp, drawing the bone horizontally toward the fracture line → producing **2.8 kN** of self-compression per hole.

## 2.5 Intramedullary (IM) Devices

An IM nail is a long rod inserted into the medullary canal of a long bone. It is the **gold standard** for diaphyseal fractures of the femur, tibia, and humerus.

### 2.5.1 Biomechanical Advantages

- **Load-sharing** implant — physiological load passes through bone and nail in parallel.
- **Central (intramedullary) position** — short lever arm → high bending and torsional stiffness.
- **Minimally invasive insertion** — closed reduction, preserves fracture haematoma and biology.
- **Allows early weight-bearing** due to its high fatigue strength.

### 2.5.2 IM Nail Generations

| Generation | Example | Key Feature | Rotational Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st (1950s) | Kuntscher Cloverleaf | V-shaped / cloverleaf cross-section | None (slot design) |
| 2nd (1970s) | Ender / Hackethal | Flexible multiple nails | Poor |
| 3rd (1980s) | Gross-Kempf, AO IMN | Transverse locking screws | Excellent |
| 4th (2000s) | PFN-A, TFN, TROCH | Proximal locking for periarticular fx | Excellent + angular stability |

### 2.5.3 Interlocking IM Nail — The Engineering View

A modern interlocking nail is essentially a **hollow titanium tube with a closed slot**. The transverse locking screws convert the nail-bone construct into a **rigid frame**, controlling:
- **Length** (proximal-distal translation).
- **Alignment** (varus-valgus and flexion-extension).
- **Rotation** (axial spin).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The biomechanics of fracture fixation is applied beyond orthopaedics: in **dental implantology** (osseointegrated screws), **veterinary surgery** (canine/feline long-bone plating), **space medicine** (osteoporosis in microgravity), and even **forensic biomechanics** (reconstructing trauma mechanisms from implant failure patterns).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Screw Insertion Torque (Mechanical Efficiency of a Helix)

A bone screw acts as a **simple machine** — an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. The relationship between applied torque T and generated axial compression F is derived by resolving forces on the helical thread.

Let:
- $\alpha$ = helix angle of the thread on the mean cylinder.
- $\phi$ = friction angle between thread and bone, where $\mu = \tan\phi$.
- $d_m$ = mean (pitch) diameter of the thread.
- $F$ = axial compressive force generated by the screw.

**Step 1 — Resolve thread normal force on the helix:**

The thread is a ramp inclined at $\alpha$ on a cylinder of radius $d_m / 2$. A force $F$ applied axially corresponds to a tangential force on the cylinder:

$$
F_t = F \cdot \tan(\alpha)
$$

**Step 2 — Add friction force opposing motion:**

The friction force on the thread surface is:

$$
F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N = F \cdot \tan(\phi)
$$

acting tangentially and opposing rotation.

**Step 3 — Total tangential force on cylinder:**

$$
F_{total} = F \cdot \tan(\alpha) + F \cdot \tan(\phi) = F \cdot \tan(\alpha) + F \cdot \tan(\phi)
$$

But because the resultant friction angle and helix angle add vectorially on the inclined plane:

$$
F_{total} = F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute torque from tangential force at radius $d_m/2$:**

$$
T = F_{total} \cdot \frac{d_m}{2} = F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \frac{d_m}{2}
$$

**Final Equation (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\,T = F \cdot \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \frac{d_m}{2}\,}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is why **self-tapping screws** with sharper, deeper threads (larger $\alpha$) and **HA-coated screws** (low $\phi$) generate higher compression with less torque — a direct application of the helical wedge principle.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pull-Out Strength of a Cortical Screw

Pull-out strength is the tensile force required to strip the bone thread from the cortex. It depends on the cylindrical **shear surface** left behind when the thread strips.

**Step 1 — Identify the shear surface area:**

When a screw of outer diameter $d_o$ and core diameter $d_c$ is pulled out of bone, the cylindrical bone "thread" shears along a surface whose circumference is $\pi \cdot d_c$ (the minor diameter) and height is the thread length $L$.

$$
A_{shear} = \pi \cdot d_c \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Apply shear-stress limit:**

If $\tau_{bone}$ is the shear strength of the bone (cortical $\approx 60$ MPa; cancellous $\approx 5$–10 MPa):

$$
F_{po} = \tau_{bone} \cdot A_{shear} = \tau_{bone} \cdot \pi \cdot d_c \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical form used clinically (Chapman, Tencer, etc.):**

Combining the geometric factor with bone-quality empirical constant $K$:

$$
\boxed{\,F_{po} = K \cdot L \cdot d_o\,}
$$

> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> A 4.5 mm cortical screw engages **16 mm** of cortical bone. With $K_{cortical} = 25$ N/mm²:
>
> $$F_{po} = 25 \times 16 \times 4.5 = 1800 \text{ N} \approx 1.8 \text{ kN}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bending Stiffness of a 4-Point Bending Plate (DCP/LCP)

A plate spans a fracture of length $L_f$ and is loaded by muscle/tissue forces equivalent to a distributed load $w$ (N/m). For analytical simplicity, treat the plate as a **simply supported beam** of length $L$ under 3-point bending:

**Step 1 — Plate as a beam:**

Bending stiffness is defined as:

$$
K_{plate} = \frac{48 \cdot E \cdot I}{L^{3}}
$$

for a centrally loaded simply supported beam of length $L$.

**Step 2 — Substitute rectangular cross-section moment of inertia:**

For a plate of width $b$ and thickness $h$:

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,K_{plate} = \frac{4 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{L^{3}}\,}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Plate stiffness scales with the **cube of its thickness** — doubling plate thickness from 3.5 mm to 7.0 mm makes it **8×** stiffer, but also heavier and more stress-shielding.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Load Sharing Between IM Nail and Bone

An IM nail and the surrounding cortical bone act as **two parallel springs** sharing the same axial load $P$.

**Step 1 — Define stiffnesses:**

Bone axial stiffness: $K_{bone} = \dfrac{E_b \cdot A_b}{L}$

Nail axial stiffness: $K_{nail} = \dfrac{E_n \cdot A_n}{L}$

**Step 2 — Compatibility (same strain) and equilibrium:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{P_b}{E_b A_b} = \frac{P_n}{E_n A_n}
$$

$$
P = P_b + P_n
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for load fraction in bone:**

$$
P_b = P \cdot \frac{K_{bone}}{K_{bone} + K_{nail}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\%\,Load_{bone} = \frac{K_{bone}}{K_{bone} + K_{nail}} \times 100\%\,}
$$

> **Numerical Example:**
> $E_b = 18$ GPa, $A_b = 350$ mm²; $E_n = 110$ GPa (Ti), $A_n = 60$ mm²; $L$ cancels.
>
> $$K_{bone} = 18{,}000 \times 350 = 6.3 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}$$
>
> $$K_{nail} = 110{,}000 \times 60 = 6.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ N}$$
>
> $$\%\,Load_{bone} = \frac{6.3}{6.3 + 6.6} \times 100\% \approx 48.8\%$$
>
> Hence, ~50% load passes through the nail, and ~50% through the bone — confirming the **load-sharing** classification.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Euler Buckling Load of a Slender IM Nail

If the IM nail is slender (slenderness ratio > critical), it can buckle inside the medullary canal. For a column pinned at both ends (effective length factor $K = 1$):

**Step 1 — Euler equation:**

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $I = \pi d^{4}/64$:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{3} \cdot E \cdot d^{4}}{64 \cdot (K \cdot L)^{2}}\,}
$$

> **Critical Slenderness Check:** The nail is in the Euler regime only if $\lambda > \lambda_{critical} = \pi \sqrt{E/\sigma_y}$. For titanium nails in femoral canals, $\lambda$ is typically below this threshold, so they fail by **material yielding**, not buckling.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Device Selection by Fracture Type

| Fracture Pattern | Recommended Device | Biomechanical Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Transverse diaphyseal (femur/tibia) | Compression DCP or IM nail | Interfragmentary compression promotes primary healing |
| Short oblique (< 30°) | Lag screw + neutralization plate | Lag screw creates compression; plate neutralises bending |
| Spiral (long oblique) | Multiple lag screws or cerclage + plate | Compression along spiral line |
| Comminuted diaphyseal | Bridge plate or interlocking IM nail | Preserves biology, restores length/alignment |
| Metaphyseal (e.g., distal radius) | Buttress plate | Resists axial collapse |
| Patella / olecranon | Tension-band wiring (K-wires + wire) | Converts tensile pull into compression |
| Paediatric long-bone | Titanium Elastic Nail (TEN) | Flexible, avoids growth-plate damage |
| Osteoporotic periarticular | Locking Compression Plate (LCP) | Fixed-angle stability in weak bone |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEBT612]**
**Define "Load-Sharing" and "Load-Bearing" orthopaedic implants. Give two clinical examples of each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **load-sharing implant** transfers a portion of the physiological load to the bone at the fracture site, allowing simultaneous loading of both implant and bone. It is preferred when the bone has good biological healing potential.

- *Examples:* Intramedullary nail, external fixator, tension-band wire.

A **load-bearing implant** carries the majority of the physiological load, with minimal load transferred to the bone. It is used when the bone is osteoporotic, comminuted, or biologically compromised.

- *Examples:* Long bridging plate, blade plate, total hip prosthesis.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEBT612]**
**Differentiate between cortical and cancellous screws. State one clinical indication for each.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cortical Screw | Cancellous Screw |
|---|---|---|
| Thread pitch | Fine (small) | Coarse (large) |
| Thread depth | Shallow | Deep |
| Outer diameter | 3.5 / 4.5 mm | 4.0 / 6.5 mm |
| Bone type engaged | Dense diaphyseal cortex | Soft metaphyseal cancellous bone |
| Clinical indication | Diaphyseal long-bone fractures (e.g., femoral shaft) | Epiphyseal/metaphyseal fractures (e.g., tibial plateau, femoral neck) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Pattern — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEBT612] (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the design and biomechanical working of a Dynamic Compression Plate (DCP). Derive the expression for the interfragmentary compression generated by its spherical screw-hole geometry.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the biomechanical principles of an interlocking intramedullary nail. Compare its load-sharing behaviour with that of a compression plate.** **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a) — DCP Design and Compression Generation**

**Design Features of the DCP (introduced by the AO/ASIF group, 1969):**
- Material: 316L stainless steel (modulus ≈ 200 GPa) or titanium alloy (Ti-6Al-4V, E ≈ 110 GPa).
- Thickness: 3.5 mm (small fragment) or 4.5 mm (large fragment).
- The screw hole is **not circular** — it is a **sloped, inclined cylindrical surface** shaped like an oblique cone with a horizontal floor (the so-called "spherical-glide" or "sloped hole" geometry).
- The screw head is hemispherical and matches the slope of the hole.

**Biomechanical Working — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** The screw is inserted in the *load* (eccentric) position of the DCP hole, i.e., the hole farthest from the fracture line.

**Step 2:** As the screw is tightened, the hemispherical head rolls down the sloped wall of the hole. This downward sliding translates into a **horizontal displacement** of the screw, plate, and the attached bone fragment toward the fracture line.

**Step 3:** This horizontal movement generates a **compressive axial force** at the fracture site.

**Derivation of Compression Force:**

Let $\theta$ be the inclination angle of the hole's slope. The downward vertical displacement $d_v$ of the screw head produces horizontal displacement $d_h$:

$$
d_h = d_v \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

For a typical AO DCP, $\theta \approx 25°$ and $d_v \approx 1.0$ mm:

$$
d_h = 1.0 \cdot \tan(25°) \approx 0.466 \text{ mm}
$$

The corresponding axial compression force $F_c$ generated in the bone, assuming elastic bone of axial stiffness $K_b$:

$$
F_c = K_b \cdot d_h
$$

Empirically (Perren strain theory), this corresponds to a pre-load of approximately **2.5 – 2.8 kN** per loaded DCP hole, sufficient to produce a compressive strain > 2% — the threshold for *primary* bone healing.

**Conclusion:** The DCP converts rotational screw torque into transverse bone translation, producing controlled interfragmentary compression without the need for a separate tensioning device.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Interlocking IM Nail Biomechanics**

**Definition:** An interlocking IM nail is a long, hollow, closed-section rod (Ti-6Al-4V, E ≈ 110 GPa) inserted into the medullary canal, fixed proximally and distally by transverse locking screws that pass through holes in the nail and the adjacent cortices.

**Biomechanical Functions:**
1. **Length control** — distal/proximal screws prevent telescoping.
2. **Rotational control** — transverse screws prevent axial spin.
3. **Alignment control** — nail maintains varus-valgus and AP alignment.
4. **Load sharing** — physiological axial load passes through the bone–nail composite.

**Load-Sharing Equation:**

The IM nail and cortical bone act as **two parallel springs** of equal length $L$:

$$
K_{bone} = \frac{E_b \cdot A_b}{L}, \quad K_{nail} = \frac{E_n \cdot A_n}{L}
$$

Load fractions:

$$
P_{bone} = P \cdot \frac{K_{bone}}{K_{bone} + K_{nail}}, \quad P_{nail} = P \cdot \frac{K_{nail}}{K_{bone} + K_{nail}}
$$

**Numerical Comparison (Femoral IM Nail vs DCP):**

| Parameter | IM Nail | DCP |
|---|---|---|
| Position | Intramedullary (central) | Extramedullary (eccentric) |
| Lever arm | Short | Long |
| Mode | Load-sharing | Load-bearing |
| Bending stiffness (relative) | Moderate | High |
| Stress shielding risk | Low | Higher |
| Typical use | Diaphyseal shaft fracture | Diaphyseal / metaphyseal fracture |

**Conclusion:** The IM nail's central position yields a much smaller bending lever arm than an eccentric plate, making it intrinsically more efficient in bending and torsion. Its load-sharing nature preserves physiological bone loading and reduces stress shielding, while the locking screws provide rotational and length stability.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OEEBT612] (14 Marks)**
